feedback.error: Unable to sign in right now.

form.description: Enter your administrator credentials to continue into the PUG operations workspace.
form.title: Login

adminOnly.description: The login entry point is reserved for the admin workspace and protected routes.
adminOnly.title: Administrator-only access

refresh.description: Expired access tokens are refreshed through the middleware before the shell is interrupted.
refresh.title: Automatic token refresh

session.description: Secure cookies keep the session stable across the protected application area.
session.title: Session continuity

hero.title: Sign in to keep academic operations controlled and consistent.

noContent.description: There is no content to show in this area yet.

notFound.description: The requested record could not be found.

compactCard.description: A denser accordion still works if each item remains easy to scan and the labels stay explicit.

escalation.description: Keep the label decisive so users can tell what they will reveal before opening the item.
escalation.title: Escalation path

ownership.description: The body should answer one narrow question instead of becoming a page hidden behind a trigger.
ownership.title: Ownership rules

timing.description: Use the compact form for short operational guidance and support answers.
timing.title: Response timing

compactCard.title: Compact disclosure

accordion.description: Accordion is the secondary-disclosure primitive for stacked content inside the current page flow. Use it when details should stay nearby without competing as primary layout or primary navigation.

multiCard.description: Allow multiple items to remain open only when comparison or simultaneous review is genuinely useful.

filters.description: Useful when users need to inspect several configuration groups at the same time.
filters.title: Saved filters

history.description: Use it when independent sections may all deserve attention during the same task.
history.title: Change history

visibility.description: Multi-open mode reduces unnecessary toggling when the user is reviewing related details.
visibility.title: Visibility rules

multiCard.title: Multiple open sections

items.disclosure: Use accordion when each section can be understood from its trigger label before the body is opened.
items.multi: Use `type="single"` when sections should compete for attention. Use `type="multiple"` when users may need to compare or keep several sections open together.
items.secondary: Treat accordion as secondary disclosure inside sections, cards, and settings groups. Do not use it as primary navigation or primary page switching.
items.trigger: Trigger labels should be short and descriptive enough for users to decide whether the section is relevant before expanding it.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Accordion type="single" collapsible>
  <AccordionItem value="overview">
    <AccordionTrigger>Overview</AccordionTrigger>
    <AccordionContent>
      Secondary supporting details
    </AccordionContent>
  </AccordionItem>
</Accordion>

compact.description: Use the same primitive for short operational guidance and dense supporting notes inside an existing card or section.
compact.title: Compact and multi-open usage

multiple.description: Multi-open mode is for review and comparison cases where several supporting groups may need to stay visible at once.
multiple.title: Multiple open sections

single.description: Single-open accordion is the default when one section should lead and neighboring sections should recede.
single.title: Single disclosure

singleCard.description: This is the baseline accordion pattern for supporting detail inside the current page surface.
singleCard.title: Default stacked disclosure

delivery.description: Longer explanatory content can live inside the expanded panel as long as the trigger still represents a single topic.
delivery.title: Delivery notes

overview.description: Keep the highest-level summary behind a short trigger when the page already has competing content around it.
overview.title: Project overview

roles.description: Use an accordion item when supporting ownership details should be reachable without permanently occupying the layout.
roles.title: Roles and owners

accordion.title: Accordion

danger.description: Use danger for destructive or irreversible actions.
danger.title: Danger confirmation

default.description: Brand primary action with no overhead label by default.
default.title: Default confirmation

success.description: Use success when the confirmation represents a positive commit or completion step.
success.title: Success confirmation

warning.description: Use warning when the action is meaningful and cautionary, but not fully destructive.
warning.title: Warning confirmation

alertDialog.description: Alert dialog follows one fixed confirmation pattern: required title, optional short supporting description, variant-controlled overhead, and a footer with exactly two actions.

danger.description: Deleting permanently removes this record and cannot be undone.
danger.title: Delete this record?

default.description: Confirm to apply the current update to this record.
default.title: Submit these changes?

success.description: Confirm to finalize this workflow and keep the resulting state visible to the team.
success.title: Mark this workflow complete?

warning.description: Archiving removes this record from the active workflow while preserving its history.
warning.title: Archive this record?

items.actions: The footer always contains exactly two buttons: a neutral `secondary` cancel button and a confirm button in the semantic tone of the variant.
items.fixed: Alert dialog is a fixed confirmation surface. Do not use a free-form body, custom footer layouts, or arbitrary button combinations.
items.overhead: Variants control the overhead label: none for default, Positive Action for success, Cautionary Action for warning, and Destructive Action for danger.
items.severity: Use the variant to match the action tone: default, success, warning, or danger.

patternNotes.snippet:
<AlertDialog open={open} onOpenChange={setOpen}>
  <AlertDialogContent variant="danger">
    <AlertDialogHeader>
      <AlertDialogTitle>Delete record?</AlertDialogTitle>
      <AlertDialogDescription>
        This action cannot be undone.
      </AlertDialogDescription>
    </AlertDialogHeader>
    <AlertDialogFooter
      cancelLabel="Cancel"
      actionLabel="Delete record"
    />
  </AlertDialogContent>
</AlertDialog>

variants.description: All alert dialogs use the same layout. Only the semantic variant and copy change.
variants.title: Confirmation variants

alertDialog.title: Alert Dialog

brand.description: Useful for featured entities, promoted filters, or product-owned categories.
brand.label: Featured
brand.title: Brand highlight

danger.description: Use danger badges sparingly for blocked, failed, or high-risk states.
danger.label: Blocked
danger.title: Risk signal

success.description: A compact positive status inside cards, rows, and detail views.
success.label: Approved
success.title: Healthy state

warning.description: A cautionary state that should remain visible without becoming a destructive alert.
warning.label: Review soon
warning.title: Needs attention

badge.description: Badges communicate compact status, category, or lifecycle metadata without taking over the layout. Use them when a short label should stay visually distinct inside cards, lists, and detail headers.

items.compact: Keep badge copy short. If the content needs a sentence, helper text, or multiple actions, it is no longer a badge.
items.status: Use badge for short status-like metadata such as active state, review stage, compact category, or risk level. It should stay compact and glanceable.
items.tone: Choose tone based on meaning, not decoration. The color should reinforce semantic state without carrying the entire message alone.
items.variant: Choose tone first and use variant only to tune emphasis inside the current surface. `primary` is solid in light mode and soft in dark mode. `secondary` is the outline treatment.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Badge tone="success" variant="primary">Approved</Badge>
<Badge tone="success" variant="secondary">Approved</Badge>

tones.description: Badges are most useful when they reinforce state or lifecycle meaning with compact, consistent visual treatment.
tones.title: Semantic tones

variants.description: Use `primary` for the default emphasized badge. It renders solid in light mode and soft in dark mode. Use `secondary` when the badge should stay outlined.
variants.title: Variant emphasis

badge.title: Badge

actionOrderCard.description: In multi-action rows, place the main action at the far right and keep supporting decisions to its left.
actionOrderCard.title: Right-most primary action

defaultCard.description: These examples show the stricter contract: default button, explicit primary, and the default grey secondary action.
defaultCard.title: Variant-driven defaults

button.description: Buttons trigger user actions. The contract is now stricter: `variant="primary"` is the main flat action, `variant="secondary"` is the lower-emphasis ghost action, and `usage` changes the color when the meaning changes.

items.actionOrder: In grouped actions, the primary action always belongs on the far right. Supporting and fallback actions stack to the left in decreasing emphasis.
items.default: The default button already defines the main contract: `variant="primary"`, `usage="primary"`, and `size="md"`. Use that baseline unless there is a clear reason to change emphasis.
items.icon: For icon-only buttons, always provide a readable `title`, `aria-label`, or `tooltipContent` so the component exposes an accessible name.
items.primaryVariant: Use `variant="primary"` for the main action in the set. Change `usage` only when the semantic color meaning changes, not to redefine the hierarchy of the button.
items.secondaryVariant: Use `variant="secondary"` for supporting or dismissive actions. Without an explicit `usage`, it should remain neutral and grey.

actionOrder.description: Primary actions stay on the far right. Secondary and fallback actions stack to the left in decreasing emphasis.
actionOrder.title: Action order

default.description: Start with the default primary button before reaching for overrides. Secondary intent should be expressed through the variant, not by ad hoc styling.
default.title: Default contract

primaryVariant.description: Primary variant is the filled action style. Keep it for the right-most main action and change `usage` only when the semantic intent changes.
primaryVariant.title: Primary variant colors

scale.description: Keep medium as the standard size. Move to small, large, or icon only when layout density truly requires it.
scale.title: Scale

secondaryVariant.description: Secondary variant is the ghost action style. It is the default fallback for lower-emphasis actions and supporting controls.
secondaryVariant.title: Secondary variant colors

states.description: Use the built-in states to keep feedback and interaction rules consistent.
states.title: States

button.title: Buttons

danger.description: Destructive action for irreversible changes and deletion.
danger.label: Revoke access
danger.name: Danger

info.description: Guidance action when you need emphasis without implying risk.
info.label: View details
info.name: Info

primary.description: Default high-emphasis action for confirm and submit flows.
primary.label: Save changes
primary.name: Primary

secondary.description: Neutral action for companion paths and supporting controls.
secondary.label: Export report
secondary.name: Secondary

success.description: Positive action for approvals, completion, or confirmation.
success.label: Mark approved
success.name: Success

warning.description: Cautionary action that deserves review before continuing.
warning.label: Review issue
warning.name: Warning

primary.description: Filled pattern for the main action.
primary.name: Primary

actions.body: Keep footer actions tied to the content of that card. If actions belong to the entire page or section, place them outside the card instead.
actions.description: Some cards need a footer because they collect card-level decisions or compact metadata.
actions.title: Card with actions

summary.description: Cards can also hold compact dashboards and metadata without needing a footer.
summary.title: Summary card

card.description: Cards group related content into a bounded surface. Use them as layout containers for summaries, supporting actions, and content blocks that should read as a single unit.

compact.body: Use the footer for compact metadata such as badges or icons when the information still belongs to that single card.
compact.description: Cards do not need to be tall. Compact content works when the boundary still improves scanability.
compact.title: Compact marker

list.description: A card can contain multiple internal rows when they all belong to the same summary or task.

actions.description: If a row needs an action, it should still feel like part of the same card.
actions.title: Actions stay scoped

composition.description: Keep spacing and internal hierarchy consistent across all rows.
composition.title: Composition

overview.description: Use a shared surface when rows are part of the same conceptual block.
overview.title: Overview

list.title: Grouped list content

items.behavior: Cards are structural primitives. Behavior such as dialogs, tooltips, dropdowns, and navigation should come from the elements placed inside them, not from the card itself.
items.container: Use cards for one bounded unit such as a repeated item, compact summary, or contained tool. Do not use cards as page structure or arbitrary wrappers around everything.
items.footer: Card footer is for card-level actions or compact metadata only. If the card has neither, omit the footer entirely.
items.structure: Compose cards from header, description, content, and footer only when those slots are actually needed. Header descriptions should stay short, and the header icon belongs on the left through the `icon` prop.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Card>
  <CardHeader icon={FolderKanban}>
    <CardTitle>Card title</CardTitle>
    <CardDescription>
      Short supporting description.
    </CardDescription>
  </CardHeader>

  <CardContent>Body content</CardContent>

  <CardFooter>
    <Button variant="secondary">Secondary</Button>
    <Button>Primary</Button>
  </CardFooter>
</Card>

composition.description: Use the same primitive for compact information blocks, bounded summaries, and cards that include supporting actions or metadata.
composition.title: Composition

layouts.description: Cards can host denser layouts as long as the content still reads as one coherent block.
layouts.title: Layout patterns

structure.description: Cards should expose a predictable internal anatomy without forcing every slot into every use case.
structure.title: Structure

basic.body: Use this shape for documentation notes, summaries, and supporting content that should sit inside a clear boundary.
basic.description: A card may be nothing more than a title, a short description, and one body region.
basic.title: Basic content block

header.body: If the body already carries enough context, do not force extra header copy just to fill space.
header.description: Use `CardHeader` when the content needs a visible title and short supporting description before the body. Pass the optional left icon through the header prop, not as manual child layout.
header.title: Header is optional

card.title: Card

city.description: Another good fit when locations or categories become too large for a plain select.
city.label: City
city.title: City search

disabled.description: A disabled combobox should keep the chosen value and context visible without suggesting that search or selection is still available.
disabled.label: Reviewer
disabled.title: Disabled combobox

people.description: A common admin pattern where people are selected from a growing list of options.
people.label: Owner
people.title: Owner assignment

combobox.description: Combobox combines a trigger field with search and option picking. Use it when users must choose one value from a defined set but the list is long enough that search dramatically improves scanning.

items.metadata: Options may carry a short description or other supporting metadata, but the list should still remain fast to scan.
items.scope: Use it for people, places, categories, and other datasets where the user benefits from narrowing the list before choosing while still landing on one selected value.
items.search: Use combobox when search or filtering is part of the interaction. If the list is short and obvious, a normal select is usually enough.
items.single: This primitive is for one chosen value at a time. If users need many selections, use a dedicated multi-select pattern instead of stretching combobox behavior.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Combobox
  value={value}
  onValueChange={setValue}
  placeholder="Choose owner"
  searchPlaceholder="Search owners"
  options={options}
/>

examples.description: Combobox is useful when the selected value should still look like a field, but the underlying option set needs quick filtering.
examples.title: Searchable selection

combobox.title: Combobox

basic.description: Use it when the user needs one calendar date and the field should stay aligned with the shared form system.
basic.label: Review date
basic.title: Basic date field

constrained.description: Min and max boundaries limit which single day can be selected and show the allowed window directly in the picker.
constrained.label: Publishing date
constrained.title: Constrained window

disabled.description: Use a disabled date picker when the selected day is fixed by the surrounding workflow.
disabled.label: Enrollment start
disabled.title: Disabled date

datePicker.description: Date picker captures one calendar date in a compact field. The UI is date-only, but the emitted value is still normalized to midnight so it remains compatible with backend datetime contracts.

items.constraints: Use `min` and `max` when one selected day must stay inside a known allowed window so the calendar communicates those bounds directly.
items.escalation: If the product later needs ranges, presets, recurring rules, or more complex timeline logic, that should become a higher-level composite component instead of stretching this baseline primitive.
items.native: Use this primitive when the product needs one calendar day and the field should still look and behave like the rest of the form system.
items.scope: The UI is date-only, but emitted values stay in backend-friendly datetime shape at midnight for the chosen day.

patternNotes.snippet:
<DatePicker defaultValue="2026-04-24T00:00" />

<DatePicker min="2026-04-20T00:00" max="2026-04-30T00:00" />

fields.description: Use the picker when the user needs one day and the control should stay aligned with the shared styling system.
fields.title: Date fields

datePicker.title: Date Picker

dialog.description: Dialogs interrupt the current flow to present focused content that needs attention without becoming a confirmation surface. Use them for notes, supporting details, and short focused reads tied to the current page.

basic.body: Use a dialog when the user needs to read or review focused supporting content without leaving the current page.
basic.title: Workspace notes

paragraphs.five: This keeps the layout stable while longer notes remain readable and bounded inside the same surface.
paragraphs.four: Longer reads are acceptable as long as the dialog still feels like supporting content instead of a full page squeezed into an overlay.
paragraphs.one: When content grows, only the body region should scroll. The header should remain fixed so the user keeps the title and close affordance in view.
paragraphs.six: If the content starts to demand editing flows, multi-step actions, or dense navigation, move it to another pattern instead of stretching the dialog.
paragraphs.three: The separator between header and body should stay visible so the content area reads as a distinct scrollable region.
paragraphs.two: Dialogs in this system do not use footer actions. They are for focused content, not confirmation hierarchies.

overhead.body: The optional overhead works well when the dialog belongs to a named pattern such as migration notes, reference context, or operational guidance.
overhead.title: Implementation guidance

overflowCard.description: Long content should stay bounded inside a vertically scrollable body while the header remains fixed.
overflowCard.title: Scrollable body

items.header: Do not use a subtitle below the title in this pattern. If extra context is needed, put it in the body or use the overhead as a small framing label above the title.
items.overflow: When content grows, only the body should scroll. The body uses the shared ScrollArea so the header remains visible.
items.structure: The structure is fixed: optional overhead, required title, top close button, separator, and required body. There is no footer.
items.useCase: Use dialog for focused supporting content, notes, and short reads. When the primary job is confirming an action, use `AlertDialog` instead of `Dialog`.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Dialog open={open} onOpenChange={setOpen}>
  <DialogContent>
    <DialogHeader overhead="Pattern notes">
      <DialogTitle>Implementation guidance</DialogTitle>
    </DialogHeader>
    <DialogBody>
      Body content
    </DialogBody>
  </DialogContent>
</Dialog>

overflow.description: Long content should scroll inside the dialog body while the header remains fixed and the close affordance stays visible.
overflow.title: Overflow

structure.description: The dialog structure is fixed: optional overhead, required title, close button, separator, and required content body.
structure.title: Structure

basic.description: A minimal dialog should provide a clear title and focused content without adding footer actions or descriptive subtitle copy.
basic.title: Default structure

overhead.description: Use overhead only when it adds a small framing label above the title, such as notes, reference, or guidance.
overhead.title: Optional overhead

dialog.title: Dialog

create.description: Use a right-side drawer for denser create flows that should stay tied to the current page context.
create.title: Create entity drawer

filters.description: Use the same right-side panel for richer filters when a small popover would become cramped or too fragmented.
filters.title: Heavy filters drawer

drawer.description: Drawers are the workflow overlay for heavier filters, creation flows, and denser subordinate work. In this system they are right-side panels with a structured header, scrollable body, and fixed task footer.

clear.description: Clearing removes all current values from the drawer body. This affects only the current draft.
clear.title: Clear this draft?

create.description: Set the initial record details before creating the new entity.

general.description: Name, classification, and the baseline values needed to open the record.
general.title: General data

ownership.description: Assign the responsible area and connect the entity to the correct campus context.
ownership.title: Ownership and campus

review.description: Keep a final compact review step when the creation flow contains more than one dense block.
review.title: Review before creation

create.title: Create entity

clear.description: Clearing removes the current filter values from this drawer and returns the panel to its initial state.
clear.title: Clear these filters?

filters.description: Adjust the active list view with broader filtering criteria without leaving the workspace.

advanced.description: Reserve the deeper accordion groups for less frequent criteria so the drawer stays scannable.
advanced.title: Advanced filters

campus.description: Use secondary grouped controls for broader narrowing dimensions that do not need to stay visible all the time.
campus.title: Campus

status.description: Keep high-signal state filters near the top so the most common narrowing actions stay easy to reach.
status.title: Status

filters.title: Refine filters

items.body: The body is required and vertically scrollable through the shared ScrollArea. Use tabs for peer workflow sections and accordion for secondary disclosure inside the panel.
items.clear: The clear action should confirm through AlertDialog because it destroys local progress or filter setup.
items.context: Use drawer for heavy filtering, creation flows, and denser subordinate work that should remain attached to the current page.
items.escalation: If the surface becomes mostly informational, use Dialog instead. If it becomes simple anchored micro-interaction, reduce it to Popover.
items.footer: The footer is always present and task-oriented: clear on the left, primary action on the right. Do not place a close action in the footer.
items.header: The header supports overhead, required title, short description, and a top-right close button. Keep dismissal structural, not task-oriented.
items.primary: Use `info` for Apply filters and `success` for Create. The primary action should run directly; only destructive reset needs confirmation.
items.side: In this app, drawers are right-side panels. Keep the placement consistent instead of switching sides by feature.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Drawer open={open} onOpenChange={setOpen}>
  <DrawerContent>
    <DrawerHeader overhead="Project list">
      <DrawerTitle>Refine filters</DrawerTitle>
      <DrawerDescription>
        Adjust the active list view with broader criteria.
      </DrawerDescription>
    </DrawerHeader>
    <DrawerBody>...</DrawerBody>
    <DrawerFooter
      actionVariant="filters"
      clearLabel="Clear filters"
      clearConfirmTitle="Clear these filters?"
      clearConfirmDescription="This removes the current filter values."
      actionLabel="Apply filters"
    />
  </DrawerContent>
</Drawer>

examples.description: Use one right-side drawer pattern for creation and heavy filtering so the workflow stays predictable across the app.
examples.title: Workflow patterns

drawer.title: Drawer

rowActions.description: This is now the standard dropdown pattern for the app.
rowActions.title: Row action menu

archive.description: Archiving will remove this record from the active workflow while preserving its history.
archive.title: Archive this record?

delete.description: Deletion is destructive and should only happen when the record should no longer exist.
delete.title: Delete this record?

save.description: Saving will persist the current changes for this record.
save.title: Save changes?

dropdownMenu.description: Dropdown menus now follow one constrained pattern: compact row-action menus with icons on every item, a brand-accented first option, neutral supporting options, and an optional final semantic action group.

items.brandFirst: The first option is the primary row action and should use the brand accent. All regular supporting options stay neutral.
items.compact: Dropdown menu now follows one compact row-action pattern. Do not use this component as a generic navigation list or a menu for broad settings surfaces.
items.confirmation: Meaningful actions in the final group should open an `AlertDialog` before execution to confirm the user?s intent.
items.grouping: When a semantic final group exists, use only `DropdownMenuSuccessItem`, `DropdownMenuWarningItem`, and `DropdownMenuDangerItem`, in that order.
items.scope: Use dropdown menu for actions related to the current item, especially in lists, tables, and operational rows.

patternNotes.snippet:
<DropdownMenuContent>
  <DropdownMenuPrimaryItem icon={Eye} label="View details" />
  <DropdownMenuItem icon={Pencil} label="Edit record" />
  <DropdownMenuSeparator />
  <DropdownMenuSuccessItem icon={Save} label="Save changes" />
  <DropdownMenuWarningItem icon={Archive} label="Archive record" />
  <DropdownMenuDangerItem icon={Trash2} label="Delete record" />
</DropdownMenuContent>

actions.description: Use one compact row-action menu pattern across the app. The first item is brand-accented, regular support actions stay neutral, and the final semantic actions are opt-in.
actions.title: Compact actions

dropdownMenu.title: Dropdown Menu

compact.description: This queue is empty right now.
compact.title: Nothing to review

emptyState.description: Empty states communicate that content is absent and explain what the user can do next. Use them for first-use moments, empty results, filtered views, and access-related absence of content.

firstUse.description: Create the first project to start organizing work, assigning owners, and tracking progress from this area.
firstUse.title: No projects yet

items.actions: Add actions only when the user has a meaningful next step. Do not add buttons just to fill the space.
items.context: Match the empty state to the reason content is missing: first use, no results, no access, or a temporarily empty dataset.
items.message: Lead with a clear title and short description that explains why nothing is shown without blaming the user.
items.scope: Use empty states inside the container that owns the absence of content: a page section, a card, a table region, or a list body.

patternNotes.snippet:
<EmptyState
  icon={<Inbox />}
  title="No items yet"
  description="Create the first item to populate this view."
  actions={
    <Button>New item</Button>
  }
/>

noAccess.description: This user is not assigned to any workspace yet. Grant access before expecting content in this area.
noAccess.title: No accessible workspaces

noResults.description: No records matched the current filters. Adjust the criteria or clear filters to broaden the results.
noResults.title: No matching results

compact.description: Use a smaller version when the empty state lives inside a tighter region such as a card or side panel.
compact.title: Compact inline state

firstUse.description: Use a more inviting empty state when the user has not created any content yet.
firstUse.title: First-use state

results.description: Different absence cases need different tone and actions.
results.title: Results and access states

emptyState.title: Empty State

custom.description: Use a separate tooltip message when the accessible label should stay short but the visible hint needs more context.
custom.label: Preview
custom.title: Custom tooltip copy

tooltip.description: When only a label is provided, the icon uses that same value for accessibility and the tooltip hint.
tooltip.label: Search
tooltip.title: Label-driven tooltip

inline.description: Decorative icons should reinforce nearby text without competing with it in the accessibility tree.
inline.title: Inline support icon

status.description: Compact status markers often need an icon visually, but not as a separate announcement.
status.title: Status badge

icon.description: Icons communicate supporting meaning, reinforce actions, and compact visual status. Use the icon primitive so decorative icons stay silent, labeled icons remain accessible, and standalone icons can expose tooltip help when needed.

integrationCard.description: The icon primitive also works inside higher-level controls. In those cases, the parent control still owns the accessible name and tooltip behavior.
integrationCard.title: Composed with buttons

items.accessible: Provide a `label` when the icon must expose meaning on its own. The primitive uses Radix Accessible Icon for that accessible name.
items.buttons: Inside buttons and other controls, keep the inner icon decorative. Accessibility and tooltip behavior should usually belong to the parent control.
items.decorative: Use icons as decorative by default when adjacent text already communicates the meaning.
items.tooltip: A labeled standalone icon can also expose a tooltip. If no custom tooltip is provided, the label is reused as the hint.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Icon
  icon={Search}
  label="Search"
  tooltipContent="Find records"
  size={16}
  className="text-[color:var(--color-brand)]"
/>

accessibility.description: When an icon stands alone, give it an accessible label and optionally a custom tooltip.
accessibility.title: Accessibility and tooltip

decorative.description: Most icons are supporting visuals and should not create redundant accessibility output.
decorative.title: Decorative usage

integration.description: Use the same primitive for standalone icons and icons composed inside buttons or other controls.
integration.title: Integration

icon.title: Icon

disabled.description: Inputs should clearly show when they are unavailable.
disabled.label: Workspace name
disabled.title: Disabled field

email.description: A standard single-line field with lightweight leading context.
email.label: Email
email.title: Email field

password.description: Use the built-in reveal behavior when the field should let users inspect the hidden value temporarily.
password.label: Password
password.title: Password field

input.description: Input is the base text field primitive for short single-line values. Use it for identifiers, email, passwords, search, and compact form fields where the value should stay visible on one line.

items.base: Use input for short-form values that fit naturally on one line and should remain directly editable in place.
items.icons: Leading icons are useful for lightweight context such as search or email, but they should remain supportive rather than carrying essential meaning alone.
items.password: Password fields can expose a reveal toggle, but the primitive should still behave like one field rather than a separate custom pattern.
items.scope: If the content becomes long-form or multi-paragraph, switch to a text area instead of stretching the input pattern.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Input type="email" placeholder="admin@pug.edu.br" />

<Input type="password" showPasswordToggle />

fields.description: The same primitive should handle standard text entry and password reveal behavior without fragmenting the API.
fields.title: Common fields

search.description: A lightweight icon can help the field read as search without changing the underlying primitive.
search.title: Search field

input.title: Input

owners.description: Descriptions help when selected values represent people or roles, and the badge tone can reinforce the meaning of that selection set.
owners.label: Owners
owners.title: Owners and assignees

status.description: A common admin filter case where several statuses may remain active together.
status.label: Statuses
status.title: Status filters

multiSelect.description: Multi select lets users choose several values from one compact field. Use it when multiple filters or assignments belong to the same category and should stay grouped under one trigger.

items.escalation: If the option set becomes very large, searchable, or deeply grouped, evolve to a richer pattern instead of stretching the simple multi-select too far.
items.options: Descriptions help when values represent people, roles, or less obvious states, but the list should still remain easy to scan and toggle.
items.scope: Use multi-select when the user may choose several values from the same set without scattering checkboxes through the main layout.
items.summary: The trigger should summarize the current selection compactly through badges instead of trying to render a long open list in the closed state.
items.tone: Selection badges use the brand tone by default. Only switch to another tone when the set carries real semantics, such as danger, warning, info, or success.

patternNotes.snippet:
<MultiSelect
  value={value}
  onValueChange={setValue}
  placeholder="Choose statuses"
  selectionTone="brand"
  options={[
    { value: "active", label: "Active" },
    { value: "paused", label: "Paused" },
  ]}
/>

filters.description: Multi select is a good fit when several filters or assignments should stay available in one compact field.
filters.title: Grouped multi-value fields

multiSelect.title: Multi Select

popover.description: Popover is the small contextual surface anchored to a trigger. Use it for quick filters, supporting metadata, and local choices that should stay inside the current flow.

inline.description: For tiny interface adjustments, the popover can stay even leaner.
inline.title: Local choice

quickFilters.description: Apply small adjustments without leaving the current surface.
quickFilters.title: Refine list

support.description: A popover can carry helpful context without turning into an action menu or a dialog.
support.panelDescription: Short metadata and extra context stay close to the interaction point.
support.title: Supporting panel

items.anchored: Popover should stay connected to the trigger and its immediate context. It is not a loose mini dialog on the screen.
items.compact: Keep the content short, readable, and contained. Once it starts feeling like a full task, it has outgrown the pattern.
items.escalation: If it becomes a short action list, use dropdown menu. If it becomes heavy filtering, create, or edit work, use drawer. If it becomes focused reading, use dialog.
items.singlePurpose: Each popover should resolve one local intent: a quick filter, a short supporting panel, or a tiny contextual choice.

patternNotes.snippet:
Refine list

Small immediate adjustments.

quickFilters.description: Use popover when the filter is small, local, and can be applied without opening a full drawer.
quickFilters.title: Quick filters

support.description: Popover also works well for short panels of context, metadata, or local preferences.
support.title: Supporting context

popover.title: Popover

display.description: Sections, cards, tables, and visual states.
display.label: Display
display.title: Display

forms.description: Fields, selection patterns, and date input.
forms.label: Forms
forms.title: Forms

categories.description: Each primitive area should make the approved defaults, semantic variants, states, and escalation rules explicit for that part of the system.
categories.title: What these docs should teach

guidance.exploration: Primitive pages should show the approved states, loading behavior, disabled treatment, and escalation boundaries so product work does not invent local exceptions.
guidance.organization: Start by deciding whether the page needs an action, form control, display surface, navigation primitive, overlay, or structural support primitive.
guidance.patterns: These primitives are intentionally opinionated. Each page should make the default contract obvious and leave ad hoc composition as the exception.

hero.description: This library defines the approved UI building blocks for PUG Web Admin. Use it to choose the right primitive, follow the default pattern, and stay inside the intended usage boundaries.
hero.title: Shared building blocks, documented like a real product system.

rail.description: Choose the primitive area that matches the UI job you need to solve.
rail.title: Pick a primitive area first.

exploration.description: Inspect states, loading, disabled behavior, and escalation rules before you improvise a new pattern in feature code.
exploration.title: States and boundaries

organization.description: Start by choosing the primitive area that matches the job the UI needs to do.
organization.title: Areas first

patterns.description: Every primitive page should make the approved default path clearer than the optional variations.
patterns.title: Default path

actionsCard.description: Header actions should affect the section as a whole, not one individual child element inside it.

one.description: A child block that belongs to the section body.
one.title: Active records

two.description: Another child block managed by the same section controls.
two.title: Archived records

actionsCard.title: Section-level actions

section.description: Sections group related content in normal page flow. Use them to define page structure, introduce a title and supporting description, and optionally attach section-level actions.

items.actions: Section actions belong in the header only when they operate on the section as a whole. Keep the primary action on the far right and support actions to its left.
items.composition: Sections group content. Cards, tables, forms, and other dense surfaces belong inside `SectionContent` when they need more framing or behavior.
items.structure: Use sections to organize page-level content groups and introduce them with title and short description. Do not treat section itself as a decorative card.
items.surface: A section should usually remain visually quiet. If everything inside is one compact framed object, use a `Card` instead of forcing the section to become the surface.

patternNotes.snippet:
Section title Short supporting description.
...

actions.description: Actions belong in the header only when they affect the whole section. Keep their hierarchy predictable, with the main action on the far right.
actions.title: Header actions

structure.description: A section establishes hierarchy and grouping without forcing a boxed surface.
structure.title: Structure

surface.description: Sections should usually stay plain and let inner components carry stronger framing when needed.
surface.title: Sections versus surfaces

basic.body: This is the standard page-level grouping primitive. Use it to introduce content before placing denser blocks such as cards, tables, or forms.
basic.description: A section usually combines a heading, a short description, and a body region for related content.
basic.title: Default structure

minimal.body: Not every section needs heavy separation. Sometimes spacing and hierarchy are enough to keep the page understandable.
minimal.description: The structure stays useful even when the section body is small and the visual treatment is light.
minimal.title: Minimal section

cardsInside.description: Use section as the page-level grouping band, then place cards inside when the content itself needs a framed surface.

one.description: A compact framed item that belongs to the same section body.
one.title: Workspace summary

two.description: Another child surface under the same section heading and action set.
two.title: Open tasks

cardsInside.title: Cards inside a section

plainBand.body: Use the plain form when the page already has enough layout rhythm and the content does not need an extra framed surface.
plainBand.description: A section can stay visually quiet and still provide clear hierarchy for a new content group.
plainBand.title: Plain grouping band

section.title: Section

grouped.description: A grouped list can stay compact while still exposing lightweight hierarchy inside the option set.
grouped.label: Workspace target
grouped.title: Grouped workspace actions

placeholder.description: Use a placeholder when the user still needs to make an explicit first selection.
placeholder.label: Date range
placeholder.title: Unselected field

status.description: A typical admin filter where the selected value is controlled by surrounding state.
status.label: Status
status.title: Controlled status filter

select.description: Select lets users choose one option from a compact list. Use it for filters, form fields, and scoped configuration choices where the current value should remain visible in a single trigger field.

items.clear: When a value is already selected, the trigger may expose direct clearing without reopening the panel, but the control still remains a single-value select.
items.forms: Select works well for compact forms and filters. If the content needs search, richer metadata, or multi-selection, move to another primitive.
items.grouping: Group options with labels and separators when the set mixes categories, areas, or lifecycle stages that benefit from lightweight hierarchy.
items.placeholder: Use a placeholder only when no initial selection should be implied. Once a value exists, the trigger should show it clearly.
items.scope: Use select when the user must choose one value from a defined set and the current value should stay visible in the field itself.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Select value={value} onValueChange={setValue}>
  <SelectTrigger placeholder="Choose status" />
  <SelectContent>
    <SelectItem value="active">Active</SelectItem>
    <SelectItem value="paused">Paused</SelectItem>
  </SelectContent>
</Select>

filters.description: Select is a strong fit for compact filters and form fields that expose one current value at a time.
filters.title: Filter and field selection

groups.description: Use labels and separators when a flat list would be harder to scan or reason about.
groups.title: Grouped options

select.title: Select

checkbox.description: Independent choices where more than one option may be enabled at the same time.

disabled.description: This option is unavailable in the current workflow.
disabled.label: Locked setting

one.description: Send a compact digest of project updates every Friday.
one.label: Share weekly summary

two.description: Keep archived items in the same reporting scope.
two.label: Include archived workspaces

checkbox.title: Checkbox

radio.description: Exclusive choices where one communication mode or policy must be selected.

email.description: Send detailed updates to the primary inbox.
email.label: Email

silent.description: Store the update without sending an active notification.
silent.label: Silent

sms.description: Use a shorter message for urgent contact only.
sms.label: SMS

radio.title: Radio group

switch.description: A binary setting that reads clearly as on or off in the current state.

disabled.description: This preference is controlled by workspace policy.
disabled.label: System lock

item.description: Immediately allow alerts for overdue tasks and project status changes.
item.label: Enable notifications

switch.title: Switch

items.checkbox: Use checkbox when the user can independently select zero, one, or many options from a set.
items.descriptions: Checkbox and radio rows can carry a short supporting description when the choice needs one more line of context.
items.immediate: Switch should represent a live binary setting. If the user is choosing one option from a set, use radio group instead.
items.radio: Use radio group when exactly one option must be chosen from several mutually exclusive choices.
items.scope: Do not swap these controls casually. The chosen control should match the meaning of the decision, not just the amount of available space.
items.switch: Use switch for immediate on/off settings and binary preferences that read naturally as enabled or disabled.

cardExample.description: This card keeps the exact same JSX in both states. Only the `isLoading` prop changes.
cardExample.title: Project summary

skeleton.description: Skeleton is the loading primitive for content that already has a stable layout. In most cases, use it through `Card isLoading` so card slots swap to placeholders automatically.

overlaysExample.note: Open each overlay to inspect the loading state in place.

items.automatic: Prefer automatic loading states for cards. Set `isLoading` on the card and keep the real card structure in place.
items.manual: Use the low-level `Skeleton` primitive directly only when you need custom placeholder shapes outside the built-in card anatomy.
items.scope: Use skeletons in the same container that will eventually render the real content so spacing and hierarchy stay stable.
items.stability: Loading placeholders should preserve layout footprint. Avoid swapping between completely different structures while requests are in flight.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Card
  isLoading
  className="flex min-h-40 flex-col justify-between p-4"
>
  <CardHeader>
    <CardTitle>Card title</CardTitle>
    <CardDescription>
      Supporting description.
    </CardDescription>
  </CardHeader>

  <CardContent>
    <Button usage="primary">Continue</Button>
  </CardContent>
</Card>

<Skeleton className="h-4 w-24" />

skeleton.primitiveNote: Use the low-level primitive only when automatic card loading is not enough.

sectionExample.content: The section body can keep its final layout while the content resolves.
sectionExample.description: Use the same section structure during loading so the page rhythm stays stable.
sectionExample.title: Workspace health

buttonUsage.description: This is the same card structure used in the button docs, but with `isLoading` enabled on the card.
buttonUsage.title: Applied to existing card JSX

cards.description: The card primitive can now replace title, description, content, and footer slots with skeleton placeholders automatically.
cards.title: Automatic card loading

icon.description: Standalone icons can expose a simple circular skeleton when the icon itself is still resolving.
icon.title: Icon loading

layout.description: Low-level `Header`, `Content`, and `Footer` primitives also support explicit loading placeholders for custom compositions.
layout.title: Layout loading

overlays.description: Overlays can expose stable loading surfaces without inventing fake internal structure while the real content is still unresolved.
overlays.title: Overlay loading

primitive.description: Use the `Skeleton` primitive directly for layouts that do not map to the built-in card anatomy.
primitive.title: Low-level primitive

section.description: Sections can also switch their title, description, actions, and content to loading placeholders through the same pattern.
section.title: Section loading

skeleton.title: Skeleton

iconTabs.description: This variant keeps the same tab behavior while shrinking each trigger to an icon button-sized control.
iconTabs.title: Icon variant with tooltip labels

labelCard.description: Labels and separators support small but important hierarchy decisions inside forms and metadata rows.
labelCard.title: Field structure

items.iconTabs: Icon-only tabs should use `TabsList variant="icon"` and expose `tooltipContent` on each trigger so the tab label remains discoverable.
items.label: Use label to associate form text with its field directly instead of relying on nearby typography alone.
items.labelSupport: Label belongs on inputs, text areas, comboboxes, and other fields that need a persistent visible name. Placeholder text does not replace that job.
items.scrollArea: Use `ScrollArea` in bounded regions such as sidebars, panels, dialogs, drawers, and dense lists. It needs a defined height or a real height cap for scrolling to happen.
items.scrollAreaBounded: Do not use `ScrollArea` as a wrapper for the whole page. It exists to contain overflow inside an already bounded surface.
items.scrollAreaViewport: Keep inner content padding and rhythm local to the content, but leave scrollbar and viewport behavior to the shared component.
items.separator: Use separators sparingly to reinforce grouping or rhythm. They should support hierarchy, not replace spacing and layout decisions.
items.separatorRhythm: Separator works best between related blocks, metadata rows, and section transitions. Do not use it as loose decoration.
items.tabs: Use tabs when several peer sections share the same space and the user needs to switch between them without leaving the route.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Tabs defaultValue="overview">
  <TabsList>
    <TabsTrigger value="overview">Overview</TabsTrigger>
    <TabsTrigger value="members">Members</TabsTrigger>
  </TabsList>
</Tabs>

<Tabs defaultValue="overview">
  <TabsList variant="icon">
    <TabsTrigger value="overview" tooltipContent="Overview">
      <Icon />
    </TabsTrigger>
  </TabsList>
</Tabs>

scrollCard.description: Scroll areas work well inside panels or cards where longer lists should remain bounded without losing polish.

five.description: Longer collections should remain easy to scan even when constrained.
five.title: Activity item five

four.description: The primitive keeps overflow behavior explicit inside the region.
four.title: Activity item four

one.description: Scrollable regions should still preserve readable rhythm and spacing.
one.title: Activity item one

six.description: The surrounding card can stay visually stable while the list scrolls.
six.title: Activity item six

three.description: This is especially useful for side panels and compact dashboards.
three.title: Activity item three

two.description: Use a scroll area when the parent surface should stay bounded.
two.title: Activity item two

scrollCard.title: Constrained scrolling

iconTabs.description: Use icon-only tabs only when the labels are already familiar or space is tight. In that case, keep the meaning available through tooltips.
iconTabs.title: Icon-only tabs

tabs.description: Tabs are a strong fit when related sections compete for the same page space but should remain quickly accessible.
tabs.title: Tabs

history.description: This is useful when users need quick context switching between parallel views.
history.label: History
history.title: Activity history

members.description: Tabs keep related sections adjacent without forcing every detail into one long surface.
members.label: Members
members.title: Members and roles

overview.description: Use a tab when this panel is one peer among several related content areas.
overview.label: Overview
overview.title: Workspace overview

table.description: Table is the shared primitive for dense operational lists that still need clear hierarchy, horizontal overflow handling, and predictable empty and loading states.

empty.description: Pass a scoped empty state when the collection has no rows instead of leaving the region visually unresolved.
empty.title: No projects in this view

items.actions: Use `getRowActions` when the last column should expose row-level actions. The primitive owns the three-dots trigger and dropdown shell; features own only the menu items.
items.columns: Keep column definitions close to the feature that owns the data. The primitive should stay responsible for rendering, row models, and surface behavior.
items.headless: TanStack Table is used as a headless engine. The shared primitive owns the visual contract while features own their data and column behavior.
items.overflow: Wrap the table in the shared scroll area so wide datasets remain usable without shifting the broader page layout.
items.sorting: Sorting is built in for sortable columns, but it should stay quiet and operational rather than turning the table into a spreadsheet clone.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Table
  data={rows}
  columns={columns}
  caption="Operational projects"
  getRowActions={(row) => (
    <>
      <DropdownMenuPrimaryItem icon={Eye} label="View details" />
      <DropdownMenuItem icon={Pencil} label="Edit" />
      <DropdownMenuSeparator />
      <DropdownMenuDangerItem icon={Trash2} label="Delete" />
    </>
  )}
  emptyState={<EmptyState title="No rows" description="Adjust your filters." />}
/>

states.description: The same primitive should stay coherent during loading and when the collection resolves to zero rows.
states.title: Loading and empty states

structure.description: A basic operational table should feel readable before any feature-specific filters, actions, or pagination are added around it.
structure.title: Operational list structure

table.statesNote: Row actions are now a built-in last-column pattern through `getRowActions`. Keep heavier behaviors such as selection, pagination, or virtualization out of the primitive until a real feature demands them.
table.title: Table

disabled.description: Longform fields also need an obvious disabled state when the content is informational only.
disabled.label: Audit note
disabled.title: Disabled text area

feedback.description: Use a taller text area when the expected response is more descriptive and reflective.
feedback.label: Feedback
feedback.placeholder: Describe the current experience and what should improve
feedback.title: Longer feedback

notes.description: A compact long-form field for operational notes or supporting detail.
notes.label: Notes
notes.title: Internal notes

textArea.description: Text area is the primitive for longer freeform content. Use it when the value needs more than one line, such as notes, feedback, descriptions, or explanatory content.

items.longform: Use text area when the user is expected to write sentences, notes, or any content that naturally spans multiple lines.
items.resize: The field should preserve a comfortable minimum height so longer content does not feel cramped from the first interaction.
items.rhythm: Labels, spacing, and surrounding form rhythm matter even more with text areas because they occupy much more vertical space than a normal input.
items.scope: Do not use a text area for short structured values just because more width is available. Keep single-line fields as inputs.

patternNotes.snippet: <TextArea placeholder="Add internal notes" />

usage.description: Text areas are best for content that should stay readable while it grows vertically.
usage.title: Long-form entry

textArea.title: Text Area

apiError.description: You do not have permission to delete this enrollment.
apiError.title: 403 Forbidden

promise.description: {{value}} are now active for all admins.

toast.description: Toast is the transient feedback pattern for low-friction confirmations, async work, and short errors that should not take over the flow.

items.description: The title carries the main message. The description earns its place only when it adds one useful line of context.
items.neutral: Use the neutral toast by default for routine confirmations. Semantic tones are for states that need extra emphasis.
items.promise: For async work, use `toast.promise` so the toast reflects the real lifecycle of the operation instead of stacking manual loading, success, and error calls.
items.scope: Toast does not replace inline validation, dialog, or alert dialog. Use it for brief feedback that should not create a new flow.

async.description: Promise toasts should mirror the real request lifecycle. For everything else, keep the feedback short and explicit.
async.title: Async work and errors

variants.description: Neutral and semantic treatments for the most common feedback states.
variants.title: Intent variants

toast.title: Toast

danger.description: The request was rejected by the server.
danger.title: Update failed

info.description: New records are being pulled from the API.
info.title: Sync started

neutral.description: Your changes are available on this device.
neutral.title: Draft saved

success.description: The profile details were saved successfully.
success.title: User updated

warning.description: Less than 10% of the quota is available.
warning.title: Storage almost full

chrome.description: Use the chrome color variant on colored backgrounds. Items can stay compact and rely on built-in tooltip support.
chrome.title: Chrome color variant

multi.description: Use multi-select toggle groups for formatting or chip-like controls where several states may remain active.
multi.title: Multiple pressed states

pill.description: Use the pill variant when related states should feel glued together as one segmented control.
pill.title: Pill variant

single.description: Use the single toggle as the default pressed-action pattern on the standard surface.
single.label: Bold
single.title: Single toggle

view.description: A single-value toggle group is a good fit for compact view switches such as grid versus list.
view.title: Exclusive view mode

toggleControls.description: Toggle and toggle group express pressed state rather than form selection. The default single toggle uses the standard surface treatment, while toggle groups now have a constrained contract: `spaced` or `pill`, with optional `chrome` for colored backgrounds.

patternNotes.groupSnippet:
<ToggleGroup variant="pill" colorVariant="chrome" value={value} onValueChange={setValue}>
  <ToggleGroupItem value="light" tooltipContent="Light">
    <Sun />
  </ToggleGroupItem>
  <ToggleGroupItem value="dark" tooltipContent="Dark">
    <Moon />
  </ToggleGroupItem>
</ToggleGroup>

items.chrome: Use `colorVariant="chrome"` only on colored surfaces such as top bars and branded panels. Outside that context, keep the group on the default color variant.
items.defaultSurface: Use the single `Toggle` as the default pressed-action pattern on neutral surfaces. It should not turn into a substitute for checkbox, radio group, or switch.
items.group: Use `ToggleGroup` when several related states need to behave as one coherent set, whether exclusive or multi-pressed.
items.pill: Use the `pill` variant when the items should read as one connected segmented control with no gaps between parts.
items.press: These controls communicate pressed state, not form semantics. Prefer checkbox, radio group, or switch when the meaning is selection or settings.
items.scope: Toggle controls are strongest in toolbars, view switches, formatting actions, and compact filter chips.
items.spaced: Use the `spaced` variant for toolbars, view switches, and formatting groups where each item should remain clearly separate.
items.toggle: Use toggle for one pressed/unpressed control that behaves like a stateful action button.

chrome.description: Use the chrome color variant when the group sits on a branded or colored background.
chrome.title: Chrome on colored surfaces

default.description: This is the baseline pressed-action control for neutral surfaces.
default.title: Default single toggle

pill.description: The pill variant turns the group into one connected control for compact mutually exclusive choices.
pill.title: Pill group variant

spaced.description: The default toggle group layout keeps items separated. Use it for toolbars, formatting sets, and view switches.
spaced.title: Spaced group variant

tooltip.description: Tooltips provide short contextual hints for controls and inline UI elements. Use the wrapper directly around custom triggers or rely on automatic tooltips for icon-only buttons.

items.clarify: Use tooltips to clarify compact controls, especially icon-only actions that need visible context on hover or focus.
items.compact: Keep tooltip copy short and neutral. If the content needs instructions or multiple actions, use another pattern such as helper text, a popover, or a dialog.
items.iconButtons: For icon buttons, the button primitive already resolves tooltip content from `tooltipContent`, `title`, or the accessible label.
items.reinforce: Tooltips should reinforce an existing control, not carry essential information by themselves.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Tooltip content="Open details" side="top" align="center">
  <button type="button">Trigger</button>
</Tooltip>

<Button
  size="icon"
  variant="secondary"
  title="Add item"
  leadingIcon={<Plus />}
/>

bottom.description: Safer when there is no room above the trigger.
bottom.name: Bottom

left.description: Works well near right-edge controls and floating actions.
left.name: Left

right.description: Useful beside dense left-aligned navigation and stacked lists.
right.name: Right

top.description: Default placement for compact controls and icon actions.
top.name: Top

compactMetadata.description: A small amount of structured content is fine when the hint needs a title and one line of context.
compactMetadata.title: Compact metadata
compactMetadata.tooltipBody: Reference docs are kept separate from the current flow.

inlineHint.description: Wrap custom inline triggers when you need context without changing the surrounding layout.
inlineHint.prefix: Status updates are available in the
inlineHint.title: Inline hint
inlineHint.tooltip: Opens the activity stream for this workspace.

automatic.description: Icon buttons resolve tooltip content automatically from the props already used for accessibility.
automatic.title: Automatic icon tooltip

placement.description: Choose the placement that keeps the hint readable near surrounding layout constraints.
placement.title: Placement

richContent.description: Tooltip content may include small amounts of structure, but it should remain compact and scannable.
richContent.title: Rich content

triggerGuidance.description: Use tooltips as supporting guidance, not as the primary source of meaning.
triggerGuidance.title: Trigger guidance

tooltip.title: Tooltip

avoidLongInstructions.description: If the message starts reading like onboarding copy, use helper text, a popover, or a dialog instead.
avoidLongInstructions.title: Avoid long instructions
